conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me review the official eligibility rules to ensure I give you accurate guidance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I can help you assess your eligibility for Child Benefit. 

To start, could you please tell me: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit and would like to know if I'm eligible. 

I have one child - Alex, who is 18 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex, I need to understand their current situation.

**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or on an approved training course)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They finished school and they're working now - they have a full-time job at a local shop.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to complete the assessment for Alex.

**How many hours per week does Alex work at the local shop?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: They work full-time - it's about 30 hours a week.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. I need to ask a couple more questions to complete the assessment.

**Do you live with Alex in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Manchester, and Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Just one final question to complete the assessment:

**Does Alex receive any benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't receive any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the rules, I'm afraid you would **not be eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex. Here's why:

According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 (under 20), but only if they remain in approved education or training. The rules specifically state that you'll stop getting Child Benefit if your child:
- Starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training

Since Alex is 18 years old, working 30 hours per week (which is more than 24 hours), and is not in approved education or training, they no longer meet the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

Child Benefit would have stopped when Alex left education and started working full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit or any other questions I can help you with?

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and working 30 hours per week in paid employment at a local shop. Alex is not in approved education or training. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works 30 hours per week (exceeding the 24-hour threshold) and is not in education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives with their 18-year-old child Alex in the UK. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, Alex has left education and is working full-time (30 hours per week). Since Alex works more than 24 hours per week and is not in approved education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and working 30 hours per week in paid employment at a local shop. Alex is not in approved education or training. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works 30 hours per week (exceeding the 24-hour threshold) and is not in education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives with their 18-year-old child Alex in the UK. While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, Alex has left education and is working full-time (30 hours per week). Since Alex works more than 24 hours per week and is not in approved education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit.
